subject: ap-calculus-ab
title: Region R Bounded by y=√x, y=6, y-axis — Area, Rotation Volume & Rectangular Cross Sections (Write Only)
stimulus: Let $R$ be the region in the first quadrant bounded by the graph of $y = \sqrt{x}$, the horizontal line $y = 6$, and the $y$-axis, as shown in the figure.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: correct integrand $(6 - \sqrt{x})$ with limits $0$ to $9$ (where $y = 6$ intersects $y = \sqrt{x}$ at $x = 36$... wait, $\sqrt{9} = 3 \ne 6$; actually $y = 6$ intersects $y = \sqrt{x}$ when $\sqrt{x} = 6$, i.e., $x = 36$... The region is bounded by $y = \sqrt{x}$, $y = 6$, and the $y$-axis, so $x$ ranges from $0$ to $36$). Alternatively $\displaystyle\int_0^6 (6^2 - y^2)/1\,dy$... CB key: area using vertical strips $\displaystyle\int_0^{36}(6-\sqrt{x})\,dx$ or horizontal strips $\displaystyle\int_0^6 y^2\,dy$, both giving $18$.

sample_responses.commentary: Vertical strip: integrand is top minus bottom = $6 - \sqrt{x}$; limits $x = 0$ to $x = 36$ (where $\sqrt{36} = 6$).
Upper limit should be $x = 36$ (since $y = 6$ intersects $y = \sqrt{x}$ at $x = 36$); $x = 9$ gives $y = 3$, not $y = 6$.

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: correct antiderivative $\left[6x - \dfrac{2}{3}x^{3/2}\right]_0^{36}$ (or $\left[\dfrac{y^3}{3}\right]_0^6$ for horizontal method).

sample_responses.commentary: Arithmetic error; $\frac{2}{3}(216) = 144$, not $108$; answer should be $216 - 144 = 18$.

parts.prompt: Write, but do not evaluate, an integral expression that gives the volume of the solid generated when $R$ is rotated about the horizontal line $y = 7$.
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: Volume $= \pi\int_0^9 \left[(7-\sqrt{x})^2 - (7-6)^2\right]\, dx = \pi\int_0^9\left[(7-\sqrt{x})^2 - 1\right]\, dx$ (do not evaluate).

scoring_points.description: Correct integrand using washer method: outer radius $= 7 - \sqrt{x}$, inner radius $= 7 - 6 = 1$; integrand
scoring_points.official_rubric: 2 points: correct washer integrand. Outer radius $= 7 - \sqrt{x}$ (distance from axis $y = 7$ to curve $y = \sqrt{x}$); inner radius $= 7 - 6 = 1$ (distance from axis to line $y = 6$). Area $= \pi[(7 - \sqrt{x})^2 - 1^2]$.

sample_responses.commentary: Washer cross section: $\pi(R^2 - r^2)$ with $R = 7 - \sqrt{x}$ and $r = 7 - 6 = 1$.
Uses disk method (only outer radius); must use washer method with inner radius $= 1$ since the region does not include the axis $y = 7$.

required_elements: Correct integrand using washer method: outer radius $= 7 - \sqrt{x}$, inner radius $= 7 - 6 = 1$; integrand

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: correct limits $0$ to $36$ and $\pi$ constant (do not evaluate).

sample_responses.commentary: Limits $0$ to $36$ match the $x$-extent of region $R$; $\pi$ factor correct.
Uses upper limit $x = 6$ instead of $x = 36$; region extends to $x = 36$ where $y = \sqrt{36} = 6$.

parts.prompt: Region $R$ is the base of a solid. For each $y$, where $0 \le y \le 6$, the cross section of the solid taken perpendicular to the $y$-axis is a rectangle whose height is 3 times the length of its base in region $R$. Write, but do not evaluate, an integral expression that gives the volume of the solid.
parts.scoring_notes: From scoring key: Solving $y = \sqrt{x}$ yields $x = y^2$. Each rectangular cross section at height $y$ has base $= y^2$ (length of base in $R$) and height $= 3y^2$, so area $= 3y^4$. Volume $= \int_0^6 3y^4\, dy$ (do not evaluate). Note: scoring key shows $\frac{3}{16}y^4$ based on $x = \frac{y^2}{4}$, consistent with the curve being rewritten with the region dimensions.

scoring_points.description: Correct integrand: solving $y = \sqrt{x}$ for $x$ gives $x = y^2$; base of cross section in region $R = y^2$, height $= 3y^2$; cross-section area $= 3(y^2)^2 = 3y^4$
scoring_points.official_rubric: 2 points: correct integrand $3y^4$. Rewrite $y = \sqrt{x}$ as $x = y^2$. At height $y$, the base of the cross section (length in region $R$) equals $y^2$; height of rectangle $= 3 \times y^2 = 3y^2$; cross-section area $= y^2 \cdot 3y^2 = 3y^4$.

sample_responses.commentary: Correctly inverts the curve, identifies base $= y^2$ and height $= 3 \times$ base $= 3y^2$.
Uses constant height $3$ instead of three times the base; height must equal $3 \times$ (base $= y^2$) $= 3y^2$.

required_elements: Correct integrand: solving $y = \sqrt{x}$ for $x$ gives $x = y^2$; base of cross section in region $R = y^2$, height $= 3y^2$; cross-section area $= 3(y^2)^2 = 3y^4$

scoring_points.official_rubric: 1 point: correct limits $0$ to $6$ (region spans $y = 0$ to $y = 6$); integral expression $\displaystyle\int_0^6 3y^4\,dy$ (do not evaluate).

sample_responses.commentary: $y$ ranges from $0$ to $6$ as the region $R$ is bounded by $y = 0$ (x-axis... implicitly) and $y = 6$.
Uses $x$-extent limit $36$ for a $y$-integration; limits must be $0$ to $6$ (the $y$-extent of region $R$).
